items.title: South American telecommunication providers targeted with three new malware implants
items.description: UAT-9244, a China-nexus advanced persistent threat actor, has been targeting critical telecommunications infrastructure in South America since 2024. The group employs three new malware implants: TernDoor, a Windows-based backdoor variant of CrowDoor; PeerTime, an ELF-based backdoor using BitTorrent protocol; and BruteEntry, a brute force scanner for SSH, Postgres, and Tomcat servers. UAT-9244 uses
items.ai_summary: UAT-9244, a China-nexus APT, targets South American telecommunications infrastructure with three new malware implants: TernDoor, PeerTime, and BruteEntry. The group exploits public-facing applications and uses brute force tactics to gain initial access. To defend, prioritize securing remote access software and implementing robust password policies to prevent brute force attacks.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Investigation Into Years of Undetected Operations Targeting High-Value Sectors
items.description: Since 2020, a Chinese threat actor dubbed CL-UNK-1068 has been targeting high-value organizations across South, Southeast and East Asia, focusing on critical sectors like aviation, energy, government, and telecommunications. The group employs a diverse toolkit including custom malware, modified open-source utilities, and living-off-the-land binaries to maintain stealthy persistence. Their techniqu
items.ai_summary: CL-UNK-1068, a Chinese threat actor, has been conducting undetected operations since 2020, targeting high-value sectors in Asia using custom malware and living-off-the-land tactics. The group's stealthy persistence and diverse toolkit pose a significant threat to critical infrastructure. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize vulnerability management and implement robust exploit detection measures.

items.title: Middle East Conflict Fuels Opportunistic Cyber Attacks
items.description: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has triggered a surge in cybercriminal activity. Over 8,000 newly registered domains with conflict-related keywords have been identified, many of which may be weaponized in future campaigns. Multiple cases of malicious activity have been observed, including targeted attacks using conflict-themed lures, deployment of the LOTUSLITE backdoor, fake news blogs le
items.ai_summary: The Middle East conflict has sparked an increase in opportunistic cyber attacks, with over 8,000 newly registered domains potentially being used for malicious activities, including targeted attacks using conflict-themed lures and deployment of the LOTUSLITE backdoor. The real-world impact includes compromised systems and potential data breaches. The most important defender action is to educate users about the risks of conflict-themed lures and to monitor for suspicious activity.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking an Attack Chain of MuddyWater
items.description: An intrusion attributed to MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, was identified in a customer environment. The attack involved initial access through RDP, establishing an SSH tunnel, and deploying malware via DLL side-loading. The threat actor used FMAPP.exe, a legitimate Fortemedia Inc. application, to load a malicious FMAPP.dll for C2 communications. The timeline of activities revealed typos in com
items.ai_summary: MuddyWater, an Iranian-linked APT, conducted an intrusion through RDP and established an SSH tunnel, utilizing DLL side-loading to deploy malware. The threat actor leveraged a legitimate application, FMAPP.exe, to load a malicious DLL for C2 communications. To defend against this threat, prioritize monitoring for unusual RDP activity and implement robust SSH tunneling controls.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Seqrite: Advisory: Middle East Conflict & Cyber Escalation
items.description: Iran’s cyber ecosystem operates through a layered structure that combines state-directed APT groups (IRGC-linked and MOIS-linked); semi-official contractors and front entities; hacktivist personas and collectives operated by intelligence services; and ideologically aligned foreign collectives operating in parallel. The Stryker Corporation attack on March 11, 2026 marked a significant escalation: a
items.ai_summary: The threat involves Iranian state-directed APT groups and hacktivists using tactics like stealing or forging authentication certificates and defacement to target organizations. This escalation in cyber attacks has real-world impact on the security of entities with interests in the Middle East. The most important defender action is to enhance authentication mechanisms and monitor for suspicious activity.

items.title: Inside an AIenabled device code phishing campaign
items.description: Microsoft Defender Security Research has observed a widespread phishing campaign leveraging the Device Code Authentication flow to compromise organizational accounts at scale. While traditional device code attacks are typically narrow in scope, this campaign demonstrated a higher success rate, driven by automation and dynamic code generation that circumvented the standard 15-minute expiration wind
items.ai_summary: The threat is a large-scale phishing campaign using AI-enabled device code authentication to compromise organizational accounts. This campaign leverages automation and dynamic code generation to evade traditional security measures. Defenders should prioritize implementing additional authentication checks and monitoring for suspicious device code authentication activity.

items.title: Storm-1175 focuses gaze on vulnerable web-facing assets in high ...
items.description: The financially motivated cybercriminal actor tracked by Microsoft Threat Intelligence as Storm-1175 operates high-velocity ransomware campaigns that weaponize N-days, targeting vulnerable, web-facing systems during the window between vulnerability disclosure and widespread patch adoption. Following successful exploitation, Storm-1175 rapidly moves from initial access to data exfiltration and depl
items.ai_summary: Storm-1175 is a financially motivated threat actor that exploits vulnerable web-facing systems using N-day exploits, rapidly moving from initial access to data exfiltration and deployment of ransomware. The actor's high-velocity campaigns target systems during the window between vulnerability disclosure and patch adoption. To defend against Storm-1175, organizations should prioritize prompt patching of web-facing assets and implement robust monitoring for suspicious activity.

items.title: Unit42: Understanding Current Threats to Kubernetes Environments
items.description: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 explains that Kubernetes has become a prime target for attackers as its adoption accelerates in enterprise environments. Their research shows a sharp rise in Kubernetes-related malicious activity, driven less by classic container escape techniques and more by identity abuse and exposed application surfaces. Threat actors commonly gain initial access through misconfigurat
items.ai_summary: Threat actors target Kubernetes environments through identity abuse and exposed application surfaces, gaining initial access via misconfiguration. They exploit this access to steal or forge authentication certificates and escape to the host. Defenders should prioritize securing Kubernetes configurations and monitoring for suspicious authentication activity.

items.title: TA416 resumes European government espionage campaigns
items.description: Since mid-2025, China-aligned threat actor TA416 has resumed targeting European government and diplomatic organizations after a two-year operational shift to Southeast Asia. The campaigns primarily focused on diplomatic missions to the EU and NATO, using web bug reconnaissance and malware delivery through compromised accounts and attacker-controlled infrastructure. In March 2026, TA416 expanded op
items.ai_summary: TA416, a China-aligned threat actor, has resumed targeting European government and diplomatic organizations with spearphishing attacks and malware delivery through compromised accounts. This campaign leverages web bug reconnaissance and attacker-controlled infrastructure. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1598.002 (Spearphishing Attachment) and implementing robust email security measures.

items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: A supply chain attack targets Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4, introducing a malicious transitive dependency that executes during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through Node.js spawning OS-native shells. Defenders should immediately update Axios packages and monitor for suspicious Node.js activity.

The project was informed of the problem early through an issue repor

items.title: CVE-2026-5014: A vulnerability was found in elecV2 elecV2P up to 3.8.3. The affected element is...
items.description: A vulnerability was found in elecV2 elecV2P up to 3.8.3. The affected element is the function path.join of the file /log/ of the component Wildcard Handler. The manipulation results in path traversal. The attack may be performed from remote. The exploit has been made public and could be used. The project was informed of the problem early through an issue report but has not responded yet.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5014 is a path traversal vulnerability in elecV2 elecV2P up to 3.8.3, exploitable from remote, with a public exploit available. This vulnerability allows a threat actor to potentially access sensitive information or execute malicious code. Defenders should prioritize patching or mitigating this vulnerability to prevent exploitation.

items.description: A vulnerability has been found in elecV2 elecV2P up to 3.8.3. Impacted is the function path.join of the file /store/:key. The project was informed of the problem early through an issue report but has not responded yet.

items.title: CVE-2026-3256: HTTP::Session versions through 0.53 for Perl defaults to using insecurely genera...
items.description:
HTTP::Session versions through 0.53 for Perl defaults to using insecurely generated session ids.

HTTP::Session defaults to using HTTP::Session::ID::SHA1 to generate session ids using a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand function, the high resolution epoch time, and the PID. The PID will come from a small set of numbers, and the epoch time may be guessed, if it is not leaked from the HTTP Da

items.title: CVE-2025-15604: Amon2 versions before 6.17 for Perl use an insecure random_string implementation...
items.description:
Amon2 versions before 6.17 for Perl use an insecure random_string implementation for security functions.

In versions 6.06 through 6.16, the random_string function will attempt to read bytes from the /dev/urandom device, but if that is unavailable then it generates bytes by concatenating a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand() function, the PID, and the high resolution epoch time.  The PID wi

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: The Hidden Cost of Recurring Credential Incidents
items.description: When talking about credential security, the focus usually lands on breach prevention. This makes sense when IBM’s 2025 Cost of a Data Breach Report puts the average cost of a breach at $4.4 million. Avoiding even one major incident is enough to justify most security investments, but that headline figure obscures the more persistent problems caused by recurring credential

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
